title: Understanding AI Bias and Fairness

modules.title: What Is Algorithmic Bias
modules.sub: Defining bias and how it enters AI systems
modules.description: Learners explore the origins of algorithmic bias, tracing how it emerges from historical data, design choices, and systemic inequalities. They distinguish between different types of bias—sampling, measurement, and aggregation—and map how each can distort AI outputs. By the end, learners can articulate a clear definition of algorithmic bias and explain why it carries professional and ethical weight.

modules.title: Real World Bias Examples
modules.sub: Examining bias in hiring, lending, healthcare, and justice
modules.description: Learners analyze documented cases of biased AI across high-stakes domains, including resume screening tools, credit scoring models, diagnostic algorithms, and recidivism prediction systems. They assess the real-world harm caused to affected groups and identify the specific design or data decisions that allowed bias to take hold. This analysis builds the pattern recognition needed to spot similar risks in their own professional contexts.

modules.title: Detecting Bias in Systems
modules.sub: Applying fairness metrics and audit techniques to AI outputs
modules.description: Learners work through practical methods for evaluating whether an AI system produces biased outcomes, including demographic parity checks, disparate impact analysis, and confusion matrix breakdowns by subgroup. They practice interpreting fairness metrics and deciding which measurement approach is most appropriate for a given use case. The module builds critical judgment around when a system's outputs should raise concern and prompt further investigation.

modules.title: Building Fairer AI Solutions
modules.sub: Strategies and trade-offs for designing more equitable AI
modules.description: Learners evaluate a range of technical and organizational interventions—such as data rebalancing, algorithmic constraints, and diverse team composition—used to reduce bias in AI systems. They work through the trade-offs inherent in fairness approaches, recognizing that optimizing for one fairness criterion can conflict with another. Learners leave with a decision framework for advocating and planning for fairer AI within their organizations.

synopsis: Learn how AI systems can perpetuate unfair biases and discrimination in hiring, lending, healthcare, and criminal justice. Develop skills to recognize biased AI outputs and understand approaches for creating more equitable artificial intelligence.
